coredata.dc:title: Quantification of methotrexate in plasma samples using highly fluorescent nanoparticles
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Pharmaceutical and Biomedical Analysis
coredata.dc:creator: Golsanamlou, Zahra
Kholafazad-Kordasht, Houman
Soleymani, Jafar
Jouyban, Abolghasem
coredata.dc:description: A novel amine-functionalized silica carbon polymer dots (APTES-CPDs)-based sensor was developed for methotrexate (MTX) detection. APTES-CPDs core-shell structure was synthesized via a one-step hydrothermal process and were employed as an optical sensor for MTX determination due to quenching effect of MTX on the fluorescence intensity of CPDs. Under optimum conditions, the developed sensor detects MTX in plasma samples in the dual linear ranges of 10 ng/mL to 2.0 µg/mL and 2.0 µg/mL to 50 μg/mL with a low limit of detections (LLOD)) of 10 ng/mL and 2.0 µg/mL, respectively. The obtained results are demonstrated the accuracy of the designed nanoprobe which is as high as to be used for the MTX detection in the routine therapeutic drug monitoring. In addition, the established nanoprobe shows significant specificity towards MTX compared to the co-administered drugs. Other advantages of this nanoprobe are its simple approach, high selectivity and sensitivity for MTX determination in plasma media which can approve the applicability of this MTX nanosensor in real samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Silica carbon polymer dots
Methotrexate
Fluorescence, plasma, real samples

Analytical performance for the developed method for MTX sensing
Determination of MTX in spiked samples
Determination of MTX in real samples

(A) UV spectrum and (B) excitation and emission wavelengths, and (C) fluorescence spectra at various excitations of APTES-CPDs.
Calibration curve in plasma (A) and its corresponded fluorescence spectra (B).
Effects of common interferes on the determination of MTX.
Schematic illustration of preparation of APTES-CPDs.
Mechanism of action of the APTES-CPDs-based probe for the determination of MTX.
Comparison of the analytical figures of merit of the developed method with the reported for MTX measurement.

ce:simple-para.ce:sup: BSA-AuNCs; bovine serum albumin stabilized gold nanoclusters, G-CNT-GCE; graphene-carbon nanotubes-glassy carbon electrode, Au/AgNCs; gold/silver nanoclusters, m-AgSAE; Mercury meniscus modified silver solid amalgam electrode, PdAg/NG; N-graphene decorated with a bimetallic palladium–silver alloy.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Repeatability and accuracy of the developed sensor for MTX determination.
Stock solution stability and freeze-thaw test of MTX detection.
Detection of MTX in spiked samples by the developed method.
Obtained results for the determination of MTX concentration in plasma real samples using the developed method.

head.ce:title: Quantification of methotrexate in plasma samples using highly fluorescent nanoparticles

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of pharmacy, Near East University, Mersin 10, 99138 Nicosia, North Cyprus, Turkey

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of pharmacy, Near East University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Faculty of pharmacy, Near East University, 99138, Nicosia, North Cyprus, Mersin 10, Turkey

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel amine-functionalized silica carbon polymer dots (APTES-CPDs)-based sensor was developed for methotrexate (MTX) detection. APTES-CPDs core-shell structure was synthesized via a one-step hydrothermal process and were employed as an optical sensor for MTX determination due to quenching effect of MTX on the fluorescence intensity of CPDs. Under optimum conditions, the developed sensor detects MTX in plasma samples in the dual linear ranges of 10 ng/mL to 2.0 µg/mL and 2.0 µg/mL to 50 μg/mL with a low limit of detections (LLOD)) of 10 ng/mL and 2.0 µg/mL, respectively. The obtained results are demonstrated the accuracy of the designed nanoprobe which is as high as to be used for the MTX detection in the routine therapeutic drug monitoring. In addition, the established nanoprobe shows significant specificity towards MTX compared to the co-administered drugs. Other advantages of this nanoprobe are its simple approach, high selectivity and sensitivity for MTX determination in plasma media which can approve the applicability of this MTX nanosensor in real samples.

ce:section.ce:para: In 1940, methotrexate (MTX) was approved as the first cytotoxic agent used in the treatment of neoplasms and some cancers including leukemia, osteosarcoma, breast cancer, non-Hodgkin lymphoma, etc. [1]. Today, due to its immunosuppressive effect, it is also administrated in the therapy of several autoimmune diseases such as systemic lupus erythematosus, rheumatoid arthritis (RA), psoriasis, and organ transplantation [2]. High-dose MTX (HDMTX) is effective for cancer treating while low-dose MTX (LDMTX) usually has been prescribed for autoimmune diseases [2,3]. This dose difference can affect the pharmacokinetics and bioavailability of the drug, for instance, MTX should be reached approximately to 10−6 mol/L after injection (at least 6 h) to be effective on the tumoral cells [4]. In addition, because of the narrow therapeutic dose index of MTX, its consumption can lead to toxicity and the occurrence of some adverse effects such as hepatic anomaly and myelosuppresion [5]. Moreover, some MTX metabolites like 7-OH-MTX are less soluble and may cause renal toxicity [6]. Thus, therapeutic drug monitoring (TDM) for MTX is an important necessity considered in recent decades to not only ensure effectiveness but also reduce the side effects and the risk of toxicity [7].

Recently, various analytical methods have been reported for MTX determination in biological samples where among them chromatographic assays are more widely reported approaches for MTX detection. Besides, other methods including mass spectrometry (LC–MS/M) [8], electrochemistry [9], fluorescence [10,11], surface plasmon resonance spectroscopy [12], capillary electrophoresis [13], and luminescence [14] are also employed in the literatures. Nevertheless, the performance of each method depends on the limitations associated with their use such as complex operation steps, expensiveness and long analysis time [8]. Chromatographic approaches, despite their high separation ability, need specific high-cost instruments and skilled persons to interpret results. Also, electrochemical methods suffer from lack of repeatability [15]. Considering these restrictions, fluorescence sensors have been attracted widespread as a facile MTX detector with appropriate selectivity, sensitivity and easy readout [15]. Generally, clinicians need a simple, cheap, and user-friendly method or device which detects analytes with minimal pretreatment steps [16].

Fluorescent materials such as polymer dots (PDs), graphene quantum dots (GQDs), and carbon nanodots (CNDs) are promising candidates for designing several sensing and bioimaging platforms [17,18]. Despite other fluorescent materials (organic dyes, semiconductor QDs and polymers), CDs are less toxic, more biocompatible and photostable. Among them, carbon polymer dots (CPDs) have been offered some advantageous features of bright luminescence, chemical inertness and high compatibility with body fluids to expand their novel bio applications [19].

To synthesize CPDs, one of the most important precursor materials is citric acid, which is able to form CPDs directly with high quantum yield (QY) [20]. So far, various methods have been utilized for CPDs synthesis but among them, a hydrothermal method is convenient and cost-effective process [21,22] which is reported by Yuan et al. for the synthesis of core-shell CPDs structure [20]. However, more research is still needed to increase their quantum yield and efficiency. The surface-passivation of CPDs by doping heteroatoms like sulfur, silica, phosphorus, boron and nitrogen can significantly improve their optical properties [23]. Functionalization of silicon nanoparticles with appropriate agents makes them to demonstrate direct bandgap while bulk silica (with indirect bandgap) is a poor luminescence emitter [24]. Nevertheless, functionalized silicon nanoparticles make carbon dots more sensitive to pH changes and self-quenching in the solid state [25].

Herein, we prepared a novel core-shell structured APTES-CPDs based on citric acid through a one-step hydrothermal process using (3-aminopropyl) triethoxysilane (APTES) ( Scheme 1). After characterization, the amine-functionalized APTES-CPDs based platform was employed for MTX detection in plasma samples. The established probe demonstrates significant specificity towards MTX compared to drugs used in clinical treatment as combination therapy with MTX. MTX concentration is proportional to the quenched fluorescence intensity from 100 ng/mL to 50 μg/mL. The fabricated probe is able to detect MTX in patient plasma samples with acceptable accuracies.

Citric acid and phosphate buffer solution (PBS) were provided from Merck (Darmstadt, Germany). APTES was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Co. (Taufkirchen, Germany). MTX was purchased from Zahravi pharmaceutical company (Tabriz, Iran). All chemicals and solvents were provided from commercial suppliers and applied without any purification. All molecular fluorescence spectra were obtained by a JASCO FP 750 spectrofluorometer (Tokyo, Japan) which was equipped with a thermostat to control the temperature. A Shimadzu UV-2450 UV–visible spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan) was employed for UV-Vis spectrum recording. To evaluate morphology and chemical features of the synthesized particles, Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum by a Shimadzu model FTIR prestige 21 spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan), zeta potential and dynamic light scattering (DLS) by a Malvern particle size analyzer (Malvern, UK), transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images on Carl Zeiss LEO 906 electron microscope operated at 100 kV (Oberkochen, Germany) and field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) images by FEG-SEM MIRA3 TESCAN (Brno, Czech Republic) were employed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of silicon carbon polymer dots
ce:section.ce:para: Amine-functionalized-APTES-CPDs were synthesis via a hydrothermal approach, inspired from a CPDs method which has been reported by Yuan et al. [20] (as illustrated in Fig. 1). Firstly, citric acid (1 g) was dissolved in 25 mL distilled water. Then, the solution was mixed with APTES (300 μL) in a beaker and stirred at room temperature for half an hour. Next, it was transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave and was heated up to 160 ﹾC for about 5 h. Afterward, the mixture was cooled to room temperature. Finally, a dialysis bag with a cut-off weight of 1 kDa was used for removing unreacted or byproducts molecules.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Monitoring of MTX in standard and plasma media
ce:section.ce:para: An appropriate volume of APTES-CPDs was used to achieve optimum fluorescence spectra. The stock solution of MTX (1000 ppm) was provided in pure water and a certain amount was added to evaluate its effect on the fluorescence intensity of APTES-CPDs. To prepare test solutions, about 250 μL of PBS (pH=6), and 50 μL of APTES-CPDs (final volume of 2 mL) were added to the MTX spiked samples. The calibration curve has represented the concentration of MTX regarding the dilution factors. It should also be emphasized that all experiments were performed at room temperature.

The same procedure was employed to determine optimum conditions for the determination of MTX in plasma samples (with ethical committee approval code of IR.TBZMED.REC.1400.496 after filling an informed consent). After centrifugation of the samples and protein precipitation with acetonitrile (1:1); PBS (500 μL), amine-functionalized-APTES-CPDs (50 μL) and plasma (1 mL) were added to reach a final volume of 2.0 mL. Before and after MTX addition, the fluorescence emissions were recorded that difference between them is proportional to the MTX concentration in plasma samples. MTX concentration in plasma samples was precisely calculated by the calibration curve equation.

Preparation of APTES-CPDs was done during a hydrothermal process (160 ﹾC, 4 h) by adding APTES to the citric acid solution as a precursor. The structural and chemical characteristics of synthesized APTES-CPDs were characterized by TEM, FESEM, FTIR, zeta potential, and DLS. TEM images demonstrated that amine-functionalized-APTES-CPDs are dispersed in the form of spherical particles with a diameter size distribution from 10 nm to 15 nm (Fig. 1A). FESEM images approved the uniformity of the APTES-CPDs (Fig. 1B). Obtained results of the DLS technique show the hydrodynamic size of amine-functionalized-Si-CPDs is around 5 nm (Fig. 1S). This size of the APTES-CPDs confirms the obtained TEM results. The stability of the synthesized particles was demonstrated by measuring the surface charge of APTES-CPDs through zeta-potential (ζ) technique which is about 6.60 mV. FTIR spectrum demonstrated the presence of some functional groups on the surface of CPDs ( Fig. 2). The located peak at 3454 cm−1 represents the stretching vibration of N-H and OH functional groups. In addition, the peak located at around 1650 cm−1 and 670 cm−1 exhibits vibrational bands of CO and –COO-, respectively. Additionally, the FTIR spectrum of APTES and citric acid was shown in Fig. 2S. It is important to mention that the presence of amines and carboxyl groups improves the CPDs attachment ability by hydrogen and physical adsorption bonds to other molecules.

Fig. 3 A described the absorption spectrum of APTES-CPDs from 200 nm to 800 nm. Four absorption spectra were observed at 250 nm, 275 nm, 300 nm and 350 nm. Also, peaks 250 nm and 350 nm of UV region are the specific peaks for CPDs. A strong fluorescence emission peak was produced at around 420 nm, which ascribes n-π * transition. In both Si QDs and CPDs, the n-π * transition is the main responsible for fluorescence emission at around 445 nm [20,24]. However, in APTES-CPDs particles, strong fluorescence emission was centered at 420 nm, as depicted in Fig. 3B.

The fluorescence emission spectra of APTES-CPDs core-shell materials showed that these materials have an excitation-wavelength-dependent fluorescence emission (Fig. 3 C). Upon the change in the excitation wavelength from 300 nm to 330 nm, the emission peak wavelength remained at 400 nm with a decreasing trend on the fluorescence intensity. When the excitation wavelength further increased at the range of 320–330 nm, 330–370 nm, 370–400 nm, the fluorescence intensities showed decreasing, increasing and decreasing trends, respectively. While the peak wavelength was shifted to longer wavelengths after 220 nm. Although a comprehensive mechanism of excitation-wavelength dependent fluorescence emission of the APTES-CPDs remained ambiguous, however, their fluorescence emission at the longer excitations may be related to the decreased absorption and thus emissive spots on the external surface of Si-CPDs.

The QY value was measured through the ratio of the integrated photoluminescence intensities of the synthesized APTES-CPDs particles and L-tyrosine as a reference. First, an aqueous solution of APTES-CPDs and L-tyrosine was prepared in a certain concentration with an absorbance of less than 0.1. Next, the fluorescence intensities were recorded at the same concentration. Finally, QY of amine-functionalized-APTES-CPDs was reported through the following equation:
Q
S
=
Q
R
A
R
A
S
F
S
F
R
η
S
η
R
2

In which ‘‘S′ subscription refers to APTES-CPDs and ‘‘R’’ subscription denotes L-tyrosine, QS and QF are the photoluminescence QYs, A is the absorbance at the excitation wavelength, and η is the refractive index of the solvent which is equal to 1 due to the utilizing of water for dispersion of APTES-CPDs and L-tyrosine. The QY of APTES-CPDs was calculated as 7.9%.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of pH on the fluorescence of the probe
ce:section.ce:para: The dendritic amine functional groups of APTES molecules on the outer layer of the Si-APTES-CPDs could interact with the H+ ions and affects the fluorescent intensity and emission wavelength of the core. To check this phenomenon, the emission spectrum of APTES-CPDs was checked in the range of pH 2 – 12. As seen in Fig. 3S (A and B), the fluorescence intensity increased from pH 2–6 and then it was decreased up to 12. So, according to these results, the best recommended pH is 6. In addition, it is observed that the effect of pH on emission maximum wavelength was different at the tested pH range, in which a blue shift is observable from pH 2–12. By excluding pH 6, there is an increasing trend in the fluorescence emission from acidic to alkaline pHs. This may be explored by the fact that at the acidic pHs the proton concentration in solution was enhanced, resulting in penetrating the hydrogen ions to the inner layers and affecting the fluorescent core of APTES-CPDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of action and binding kinetic
ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence quenching of APTES-CPDs usually occurs by five mechanisms including static quenching, dynamic quenching, photoinduced electron transfer (PET), inner filter effect (IFE), Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET), surface energy transfer (SET), and Dexter energy transfer (DET) ( Scheme 2). As shown in Fig. 3 A and B, there is an overlap between the absorption spectrum of MTX (as quencher) and the excitation or emission spectra of APTES-CPDs. As known, MTX has three absorption spectra at 275 nm, 325 nm, and 375 nm. This phenome proposes quenching mechanisms of FRET, IFE, and PET.

FRET mechanism is rejected due to the following reasons. The amine functional groups of MTX are protonated in pHs below 4 because of the fact that pKa of MTX is between 4.5 and 5.8. The optimum pH value is 6.0 in this work, proposing a natural charge of the amine functional groups. The zeta potential of APTES-CPDs is + 6.6 mV. Thus, there is no strong electrostatic attraction effect that likely avoids MTX from adsorbing on the surface of APTES-CPDs due to the absence of a favorable attraction charge. Hence, a FRET acceptor-donor system is inaccessible in the system. IFE mechanism is rejected due to the commence of a new absorption peak after the addition of MTX to the APTES-CPDs absorbance spectrum (Fig. 3D). As seen, a peak is observed around 305 nm, proposing the formation of the new complex system. In a static quenching mechanism, a ground-state system is produced through APTES-CPDs and MTX interactions.

However, plotting of the Stern-Volmer approved that a static quenching mechanism is available in the interaction of MTX with APTES-CPDs. In this mechanism, upon increasing the medium temperature, the ground-state complex stability is declined, leading to a decrease in the quenching constant (KSV) (Fig. 3E). The Stern-Volmer equation is represented as the following equation [26]:
F
0
F
= 1 +
K
sv
Q
in which F and F0 refer to the fluorescence intensity of APTES-CPDs in the presence and absence of MTX, respectively. KSV and [Q] are the Stern-Volmer constant and the concentration of MTX, respectively. As shown in Fig. 3E, there is a good correlation between F0/F vis. MTX concertation. Since the KSV values decline by increasing temperature, the static quenching mechanism is proposed as the most feasible fluorescence quenching mechanism.

The binding constant of MTX to the APTES-CPDs could be calculated using the following equation [27]:
log
F
0
− F
F
= log
K
b
+ nlog
Q

In this equation, the n and Kb are the number of the binding sites and binding affinity constants which could be calculated by plotting log (F0-F/F) vis. log [Q]. The calculated value of Kb is 1.38 × 10 4 M−1, demonstrating proof of the existence of the binding sites on the MTX and APTES-CPDs. Also, the findings approved that the n value is 1, representing the being of one binding site for MTX molecules to the APTES-CPDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of various factors on the fluorescence of APTES-CPDs probe
ce:section.ce:para: To improve the sensitivity and specificity of the MTX detection platform, it is necessary to optimize the influence of various factors including time, temperature, buffer solution, pH, solvents, and fluorophore concentration. The effect of each of these values has been tested based on differences in intensity of fluorescence emission (∆FF0-F1) against their changes.

The major effective value for optimization is pH which was studied in the range of 2–12. As shown in Fig. 4S A. The quenched intensity (∆F) increased in pH 2–6 range and reached its maximum at pH 6, then it decreased from pH 6 to pH 12. Thus, the best condition for MTX/APTES-CPDs interactions was the pH= 6. In fact, protonated functional groups in acidic pH values lead to a weak association between MTX and APTES-CPDs. Also, these groups are inactivated or oxidized in higher values. The concentration of phosphate buffer can regulate ionic strength and leads to the enhancement of interactions between APTES-CPDs and MTX. As Fig. 4S B confirms 250 μL of phosphate buffer solution was caused to the highest intensity of ∆F.

The optimum time of incubation was reached 20 min after the addition of MTX to APTES-CPDs and however, after 20 min, the ∆F remained approximately constant. Fig. 4S C demonstrated it take 20 min for the interactions between MTX and APTES-CPDs to reach a maximum level. Despite the fact that the fluorescence emission of APTES-CPDs is higher at low temperatures, in this study maximum ∆F was observed at 25 °C (Fig. 4S D). The influence of the amount of APTES-CPDs on the sensitivity of the MTX detection probe was also described in Fig. 4S E. As seen, the ∆F value is insufficient at low concentrations which may be indicated the limited interactions between MTX and APTES-CPDs. At the higher concentrations of MTX, ∆F is enhanced and the highest value is obtained at 25 mg/mL.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance for the developed method for MTX sensing

ce:section.ce:para: As the first step for the evaluation of the developed probe, a calibration curve was set up and then other analytical performances such as dynamic range, the limit of detection (LOD), limit of quantification (LOQ), accuracy, precision, etc. are calculated via calibration curve equation. A calibration curve was plotted by various concentrations of MTX in plasma media. Plasma samples are deproteinized with acetonitrile to decrease the plasma matrix interfering agents. At the optimum condition, 0.5 mL of the plasma samples were treated with APTES-CPDs and then fluorescence emissions were reported in the presence (F1) and the absence (F0) of MTX. The calibration curve was constructed by ∆F (F0-F1) vis MTX concentration. Fig. 4A and B illustrate the obtained calibration plot and corresponded fluorescence spectra in the plasma matrix. Linear relationship is observed at two range of concentrations from 10 ng/mL to 2.0 µg/mL and 2.0 µg/mL to 50 μg/mL with calibration curve equations of ΔF = 17.91 C + 15.82 (p-value <0.001, R-square 0.996) and Y = 6.195 C + 49.44 (p-value <0.001, R-square 0.989). The lower limit of quantification (LLOQ) is about 10 ng/mL. Table 1 lists some of the reported analytical approaches for the determination of MTX [10,16,17,28–33]. Sensitivity, specificity and application in real samples are three main features of a newly developed method in biological media that must be provided. Most of the reported methods for the detection of MTX are usually provided favorable sensitivities, however, the real application of the developed methods was not checked in real media. In this work, the reliability of the developed method was checked in patients’ samples.

The most important feature for the evaluation of the performance of an analytical method is testing its accuracy and repeatability. In this way, food and drug administration (FDA) guidelines recommend the repeatability of a developed method should be tested in two forms of inter-day and intraday. Three low, med, and high concentrations of MTX were chosen for the evaluation of developed method precision which are covered all calibration ranges. As demonstrated in Table 2, the developed platform has a relative standard deviation (RSD%) for − 5.2 to − 13.3%. Additionally, the recovery range for the proposed method accuracy was from 86.34% to 94.33% which shows good agreement between added and obtained concentrations and indicates this nanoprobe is an appropriate platform for MTX determination.

The stability of pharmaceuticals in biological media is a crucial parameter. Freeze-thaw stability is a stability test that measures the effect of freezing and thawing cycles on the concentration of analytes. To evaluate this stability, two levels of MTX were added to the plasma samples and then freeze-thawed for at least 3 cycles. Then, concentrations of MTX were determined using the developed probe. Table 3 collects the obtained results, approving the acceptable stability of the MTX molecules during the freeze-thaw process [21]. Also, the stock solution stability of MTX was tested at room temperature and the results are provided in Table 3. In addition, the stability of the synthesized nanoparticles was checked during five consecutive days, the obtained results approved that stability of the prepared nanoparticles solution is acceptable in the first 48 h and after that time another solution should be provided from the stock nanomaterials (Fig. 5S). Fig. 5.

To evaluate the selectivity of the developed nanoprobe, the sensor performance was examined in the presence of other interference agents which can affect the MTX emission spectra intensity. These substances including Al3+, Zn2+, Mg2+, Na+, Co2+, Fe2+, K+, Cl-, NO3 -, and some co-prescribed drugs with MTX including folic acid, sulfasalazine, hydroxychloroquine, and doxycycline were investigated under the optimal condition in both buffer and plasma samples. In the presence of the constant amount of MTX (0.5 µg/mL) the effect of each of these interfering agents (0.1 µg/mL) on fluorescence intensity of APTES-CPDs was obtained. Also, the effect of some amino acids and proteins such as human serum albumin (1 mg/mL), tyrosine (100 µM), glycine (30 mg/mL) and cysteine (300 µM) were tested on their blood levels. Results revealed that the developed method is specific in the presents of the biologically available agents. Generally, the large parts of the agents are removed during the protein precipitation step and the tested values of agents are higher than normal or therapeutic conditions. The effect of the interfering agents on the APTES-CPDs emission fluorescence, is as low as no significant changes occur on the final signal of the proposed probe.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of MTX in spiked samples
ce:section.ce:para: The developed APTES-CPDs based probe was utilized for MTX detection in plasma samples. In this way, appropriate MTX concentrations were spiked to the plasma samples under optimized condition. After the calibration curve was set up, the unknown concentrations of MTX were calculated by the equation obtained from it. Table 4 shows the obtained results in plasma samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of MTX in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The real applicability of a developed platform must be checked in patients’ samples. To check this ability of our developed method, this method was used for the measuring of MTX concentration in the patients’ samples. Before analysis, protein precipitation steps and optimal conditions were applied and then the concentration MTX in real samples was determined using the calibration curve. The obtained concentrations of MTX in the patient’s plasma samples are reported in Table 5. These concentrations are between 11.5 and 51.6 µg/mL, however, in the concentrations out of the calibration range, the samples were diluted by the buffer solution.

A new sensitive and facile procedure based on APTES-CPDs was investigated for MTX detection. The intensity of the APTES-CPDs emission spectra at 430 nm quenched in presence of MTX depends on the concentrations of the MTX. Under optimum conditions, the developed sensor detects MTX in plasma samples in the linear ranges of 0.01 µg/mL to 50 µg/mL with an LLOQ of 0.01 µg/mL. The obtained results for accuracy and specificity are offered the fabricated sensor as an appropriate and interference-free recognition method for the MTX detection in routine therapeutic drug monitoring.

Zahra Golsanamlou: Investigation; Methodology, Roles/Writing – original draft. Houman Kholafazad-Kordasht: Investigation; Methodology. Jafar Soleymani: Formal analysis; Funding acquisition; Investigation; Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing. Abolghasem Jouyban: Project administration; Supervision; Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge the financial support by Tabriz University of Medical Sciences with registration number of 66701.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jpba.2022.114716.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material.
.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Non-hodgkin lymphoma in children and adolescents: progress through effective collaboration, current knowledge, and challenges ahead
J. Clin. Oncol.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: V. Minard-Colin, L. Brugières, A. Reiter, M.S. Cairo, T.G. Gross, W. Woessmann, B. Burkhardt, J.T. Sandlund, D. Williams, M. Pillon, K. Horibe, A. Auperin, M.C. Le Deley, M. Zimmerman, S.L. Perkins, M. Raphael, L. Lamant, W. Klapper, L. Mussolin, H.A. Poirel, E. Macintyre, C. Damm-Welk, A. Rosolen, C. Patte, Non-hodgkin lymphoma in children and adolescents: Progress through effective collaboration, current knowledge, and challenges ahead, J. Clin. Oncol. 33 (2015) 2963–2974.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Methotrexate in psoriasis: a systematic review of treatment modalities, incidence, risk factors and monitoring of liver toxicity
J. Eur. Acad. Dermatol. Venereol.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Montaudié, E. Sbidian, C. Paul, A. Maza, A. Gallini, S. Aractingi, F. Aubin, H. Bachelez, B. Cribier, P. Joly, D. Jullien, M. Le Maître, L. Misery, M.A. Richard, J.P. Ortonne, Methotrexate in psoriasis: A systematic review of treatment modalities, incidence, risk factors and monitoring of liver toxicity, J. Eur. Acad.
